content:
Some JavaScript behaviors confuse beginners and experts equally — here is the clear explanation for every one.

11 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

JavaScript has a reputation for being weird. That reputation is partially deserved and mostly misunderstood.
Most of the behaviors that confuse developers aren’t arbitrary. They follow rules — rules that are sometimes surprising, sometimes poorly designed, and sometimes the result of decisions made in ten days in 1995 that couldn’t be changed without breaking the web. But rules nonetheless. Understanding the rule makes the behavior obvious in retrospect and predictable going forward.
What follows are ten behaviors that generate genuine confusion across every skill level — behaviors I’ve watched senior developers debug for hours that resolve in seconds once the underlying rule is clear.
1. typeof null === ‘object’
typeof null // 'object'
typeof undefined // 'undefined'Every developer expects typeof null to return 'null'. It returns 'object'. This is universally acknowledged as a bug, documented in the ECMAScript specification itself as a historical artifact, and preserved because fixing it would…
